one_line_takeaway: SafeBench pairs an LLM-generated 2,300-pair multimodal harmful-query dataset (23 scenarios, text/image/audio) with a 5-LLM 'jury deliberation' evaluator that matches human judgment at Cohen's Kappa 0.89, and reveals commercial MLLMs (Claude-3.5-Sonnet best) are far safer than open-source ones, with the vision channel eroding backbone-LM alignment.
key_finding: Commercial MLLMs are substantially safer than open-source ones (avg SRI gap 20.78, ASR gap 26.38%). Best overall: Claude-3.5-Sonnet (ASR 0.7%, SRI 99.3); worst commercial: GPT-4o (ASR 3.4%, SRI 96.1), which was even edged out by the best open-source model Phi-3-Vision-128K (SRI 98.7). Worst open-source: ShareGPT4V (ASR 38.8%, SRI 69.9). JDP aligns with human judgment far better than baselines (Cohen's Kappa 0.89 vs 0.83 GPT-4 agent, 0.67 string-match, 0.46 Llama Guard), and the deliberation round raises average inter-rater Kappa from 0.37 to 0.61. Cybersecurity (CS) and Confidential-Information (CI) queries are the most successful attack categories (avg ASR 33% and 32.4%); Consultation (CO) the safest (avg ASR 7.4%). Adding a vision channel degrades safety: for all backbones except Yi, the MLLM has higher ASR than its backbone LM. Model size correlates positively with safety within a family but is not the dominant factor (Phi-3-Vision at 4.2B beats InternVL at 25.5B), pointing to training-data quality. The dataset is also an effective jailbreak seed (LPT raised ASR by up to 57.6%; SafeBench yields the highest attacked ASR of 56.2 vs FigStep/MM-SafetyBench/JailBreakV-28K).
core_contribution: SafeBench is a safety-evaluation framework for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) with two co-designed parts, both original to this paper. (1) A comprehensive harmful-query dataset built by an automatic generation pipeline: the authors survey OpenAI/Meta usage policies plus 10 state-of-the-art LLMs to derive a taxonomy of 8 major risk categories and 23 sub-categories, then have LLMs generate, score (feasibility/harmfulness/applicability, 1-10), and filter (top-100 per scenario) textual queries, and expand them into images (via an iterative LLM-description -> T2I -> MLLM-realignment loop) and audio (Parler-TTS). The result is 2,300 base text queries paired 1:1 with images and 1:2 with audio, totaling 9,200 samples across 23 sub-categories (100 per sub-category). (2) The Jury Deliberation Protocol (JDP): a panel of 5 LLM 'jurors' with distinct personas that independently judge each MLLM response (binary safe/unsafe + 0-5 threat score with chain-of-thought rationale), then engage in a collaborative deliberation round and reach consensus via majority vote (binary) and mean (score). JDP feeds two metrics: the standard Attack Success Rate (ASR) and a newly proposed Safety Risk Index (SRI), a 0-100 normalized aggregate of the fine-grained threat scores (higher SRI = safer). Note: string-matching, Llama-Guard-style fine-tuned classifiers, GPT-proxy judging, and human evaluation are surveyed as prior evaluation paradigms / baselines, NOT contributions of this paper.
threat_model: Adversary/eval setting: users (or red-teamers) issue harmful multimodal queries (text+image, and optionally audio) to an MLLM aiming to elicit unsafe content (e.g., bomb-making, malware, spyware installation, hate speech). The core benchmark measures safety under NON-attack conditions (harmful requests without jailbreak perturbations); the paper separately shows the dataset can seed jailbreak attacks (Sec 6.3). Trust assumption for the evaluator: no single judge model is trusted, so a heterogeneous 5-LLM jury is used to reduce individual-model bias and hallucination in scoring. The threat surface studied includes the observation that adding a vision (or audio) channel to an aligned backbone LM expands the attack surface and degrades the backbone's original text safety alignment.
limitations: Self-acknowledged / inherent: (1) JDP is computationally expensive (motivating the MiniBench subset), limiting large-scale routine use. (2) The dataset, taxonomy, corpus scoring, and even the image-realignment verifier are all LLM-generated/LLM-judged, so benchmark 'ground truth' inherits the biases and hallucinations of the generator/judge models the paper itself critiques. (3) The 0.89 human-agreement Kappa is measured only on MiniBench with 5 volunteers. (4) SRI/ASR direction conventions (SRI higher = safer, ASR higher = worse) require care when reading tables. (5) Audio evaluation covers only 3 audio-language models. (6) As a benchmark/dataset paper it proposes no defense or mitigation; it also publicly releases a large curated harmful-query corpus, a dual-use concern. (7) Absolute ASR/SRI numbers are contingent on the specific 5-LLM jury and their prompts.

datasets_used: SafeBench (proposed): 8 major risk categories, 23 sub-categories, 2,300 base text queries, 9,200 total multimodal samples (2,300 text : 2,300 image : 4,600 audio, ratio 1:1:2), 100 samples per sub-category
MiniBench (proposed subset): 10 sub-categories x 50 harmful query pairs, used for expensive analyses/ablations
Risk taxonomy sourced from OpenAI and Meta usage policies plus a survey of 10 LLMs
Comparison datasets referenced: FigStep (Gong et al.), MM-SafetyBench (Liu et al.), JailBreakV-28K (Luo et al.), plus Zhang et al. and Wang et al. benchmarks
quantitative_results: Commercial vs open-source average gap: SRI 20.78, ASR 26.38%
GPT-4o-mini: ASR 0.8%, SRI 98.4; Gemini-1.5-Pro SRI 97.1, Gemini-1.5-Flash SRI 97.2; Claude-3-Opus ASR 1.5%, SRI 98.5
Phi-3-Vision-128K (best open-source): ASR 1.2%, SRI 98.7 (4.2B params); Phi-3.5-Vision SRI 96.7
ShareGPT4V (worst open-source): ASR 38.8%, SRI 69.9; Qwen2-VL-2B lowest SRI 58 (2B params); InternVL SRI 82.1 (25.5B params)
Per-category susceptibility: Cybersecurity (CS) avg ASR 33% (max 68.5%), Confidential Info (CI) avg ASR 32.4%; safest Consultation (CO) avg ASR 7.4%, Inappropriate Opinions (IO) 11.5%
JDP Cohen's Kappa vs human = 0.89 (GPT-4 agent 0.83, string-match 0.67, Llama Guard 0.46)
Deliberation raises average juror Kappa from 0.37 (before) to 0.61 (after); e.g. Gemma-2-9B 0.45->0.78, Llama-3-8B 0.42->0.76
Jailbreak seeding: LPT increased ASR by up to 57.6%; SafeBench under attack reaches ASR 56.2 / SRI 40.5, highest attacked ASR vs FigStep 50.3, MM-SafetyBench 46.6, JailBreakV-28K 42.6
Parameter-size (intra-family): Qwen2-VL-7B ASR 25.8% / SRI 80.1 vs Qwen2-VL-2B ASR 43.1% / SRI 66.7; LLaVA-v1.6-Vicuna-13B SRI 87.2 vs Vicuna-7B SRI 74.1
Audio: Qwen2-Audio (audio+text) most vulnerable, avg ASR ~58% (male 58.2 / female 58.0); Qwen2-Audio audio-only avg ASR 12-15%; Mini-Omni avg ASR ~31-32%
Dataset scale comparison (Table 1): SafeBench 2,300 base queries / 23 categories / 3 modalities / 21 MLLMs vs Luo et al. 2,000/16/2/10, Liu et al. 1,680/13/2/12, Gong et al. 500/10/2/6
baselines_compared: Evaluation-protocol baselines vs JDP: String-Based Matching, Llama Guard (fine-tuned classifier), GPT-4 proxy agent (and human evaluation as reference)
Dataset-quality baselines: FigStep, MM-SafetyBench, JailBreakV-28K (compared on feasibility/harmfulness/applicability quality scores and on attacked ASR/SRI)
Backbone-LM baselines: each MLLM compared against its own backbone language model (e.g., Qwen2-VL-7B vs Qwen2-7B, LLaVA-Mistral vs Mistral-7B, Yi-VL-6B vs Yi-6B)
Image-quality ablation baselines: black / white / noise / random / vanilla (SD3-keyword) images vs SafeBench's semantically-aligned images
Jailbreak-attack baselines applied to the dataset: LPT (past-tense), PAP, BAP
